content.problemStatement:
Given a 323232-bit unsigned integer XXX, find the count of set bits in the integer.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a 323232-bit unsigned integer XXX.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, find the count of set bits in the integer.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single unsigned 32‑bit integer X (0 ≤ X < 2^32).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of set bits (population count) in the binary representation of X.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: 4 in binary is 100, which has 1 set bit.\nTest case 2: 1 in binary is 1, which has 1 set bit.\nTest case 3: 9 in binary is 1001, which has 2 set bits.
0 has no set bits.\n4294967295 is 2^32‑1, whose binary representation is 32 ones, so the count is 32.

hidden.label: single set bit at highest position

solution.approach: For each integer X we need to count how many bits are 1. The fastest way is to use the processor‑provided popcount instruction via language built‑ins (e.g., __builtin_popcount in GCC/Clang, Integer.bitCount/Long.bitCount in Java, bin(x).count('1') in Python). If a built‑in is unavailable we can apply Kernighan's algorithm: repeatedly clear the lowest set bit (x &= x‑1) and increment a counter; this runs in O(number of set bits). The overall complexity is O(T) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – each test case is processed in O(1) time using a built‑in popcount (or O(k) where k is the number of set bits with Kernighan's method).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
